items.title: Definition Critical: EPA Approves Three New Pesticides that May — (or May Not) — be PFAS
items.abstract: The Environmental Protection Agency on June 30 approved three new pesticides that under certain definitions — (but not the EPA’s) — would constitute PFAS chemicals. The agency approved the pesticides’ registration under section 3(c)(5) of the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and R

items.title: Unpacking the Consequences of the Decision in Monsanto Company v. Durnell
items.abstract: For more than a decade, litigation over glyphosate-based herbicides has sat at the intersection of federal pesticide regulation, state tort law, scientific risk assessment, and broader debates over the role of juries in evaluating product safety....By: Bergeson & Campbell, P.

items.title: North Carolina’s Statewide PFAS Discharge and Biosolids Studies
items.abstract: North Carolina’s new Water Safety Act launches a statewide effort to identify the sources of PFAS (“forever chemicals”) discharges into surface waters from direct dischargers, indirect dischargers and biosolids land application sites....By: Williams Mullen

items.title: Down the Drain but Not Forgotten: PFAS Compliance in Wastewater
items.abstract: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances in wastewater are among the most significant and fastest-moving emerging environmental compliance challenges manufacturers will confront.  This article first appeared on CBIA's website, July 7, 2026....By: Shipman & Goodwin LLP

items.title: Connecticut PFAS Consumer Product Reporting and Labeling Requirements Are Now in Effect
items.abstract: Key Takeaways -  What Happened: Connecticut’s labeling requirements for certain consumer products that contain intentionally added PFAS went into effect on July 1, 2026. The Department of Energy and Environmental Protection (DEEP) also published its PFAS Reporting Form for Manufa

items.title: IRS Adds Two Substances to List of Chemical Substances Subject to Superfund Excise Tax
items.abstract: The Internal Revenue Service (IRS) published a notice of determinations on June 26, 2026, that the list of taxable substances is modified to include the following two substances: chloro-isobutene-isoprene rubber and ethylene-propylene-dicyclopentadiene rubber. 91 Fed. Reg. 38778.
